title: Climate change reshapes resource adequacy risks and optimal renewable energy siting in wind and solar energy systems - Nature Energy
text:
AbstractDeep decarbonization through weather-dependent wind and solar can introduce resource adequacy risks. Climate change compounds these risks by simultaneously reshaping renewable supply and electricity demand in synergistic, region-specific ways. Here we show that long-term adequacy challenges in decarbonized grids arise from the interplay between meteorological conditions and system design, driven by prolonged renewable generation shortfalls tied to fine-scale infrastructure siting choices. Using 12-km climate projections with county-level power system optimization for New England and Texas, we find that climate change can increase resource inadequacy frequency up to fivefold by mid-century as rising cooling demand intersects with renewable resource declines or transmission bottlenecks. However, climate-informed planning provides cost-effective mitigation: Texas maintains adequacy at near-zero cost by pivoting wind capacity westward, while New England requires a modest 2.34% investment increase via solar and transmission expansion near load centres. Our findings demonstrate that achieving climate-resilient, decarbonized grids requires moving beyond aggregate capacity goals towards high-resolution, climate-informed spatial planning that reveals localized siting opportunities.

Data availability

The 12-km, hourly wind and solar generation profiles used in this study are available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.10214348 (ref. 32) and the ISO-level demand profiles are available via GitHub at https://github.com/IMMM-SFA/burleyson-etal_2024_applied_energy. The ISONE and ERCOT shapefiles were created by filtering county boundaries according to the county lists available from ISONE at https://www.iso-ne.com and from ERCOT at https://www.ercot.com. Source data are provided with this paper.
Code availability

All the codes and models supporting this paper are available via GitHub at https://github.com/lyqiuksfe/Qiu-et-al-2026 and the Transmission-Network-Aggregator toolkit is available via GitHub at https://github.com/swang22/Transmission-Network-Aggregator.

The capacities represent the mean across the 45-member ensemble. The left column shows ISONE and the right shows ERCOT. Basemaps from GADM (https://gadm.org).Source dataExtended Data Fig. 2 Climate change impacts on resource adequacy under SSP245 and SSP585 emission pathways.All panels are derived from n = 45 historically-/future- optimized systems for a, ISONE and b, ERCOT. Annual RA failure frequency (top left) and duration (bottom left) trends for historically-optimized systems evaluated under out-of-sample historical (2010-2019, HIST_HIST) and future (2020-2059; orange for HIST_SSP585 and green for HIST_SSP245) dispatch simulations. Solid lines show ensemble medians, shaded bands the 10th-90th percentile range, and black dashed lines indicate Theil-Sen trends with two-sided Mann-Kendall test. Side boxplots compare historical and mid-century (2050-2059) periods (center: median; box: 25th–75th percentile; whiskers: 10th–90th percentile). The pink box shows the out-of-sample dispatch simulation result of future-optimized systems (SSP585_SSP585: designed using 2-year subset during 2050-2059 and tested using the hold-out set of the other 8 years within the planning period).Source dataSupplementary informationSupplementary Information (download PDF )Supplementary Figs. 1–10, Notes 1–4 and Tables 1–5.Source dataSource Data Figs. 1–4 and Extended Data Figs. 1 and 2.
